mappings.rationale: Directly covers the historical development of mathematics from ancient through modern times, the central topic of section A.
Provides an overview of mathematical concepts and foundations, relevant to both historical development (A) and foundational questions (B).
Section A.2.a covers numeral systems from Egyptian to positional systems, and B.2 discusses cardinal number and set theory foundations.
Section A.2.e covers the history of algebra from Babylonian through modern European developments including algebraic equations.
Section A.2.d covers the historical development of geometry including Euclidean, non-Euclidean, projective, and differential geometry.
Trigonometry is part of the historical development of geometry and mathematical analysis covered in A.2.d.
Section A.3 covers the historical development of probability theory, including the abstract calculus and alternative views of probability.
Section A.3.b covers the development of mathematical statistics including stochastic processes and control theory.
Infinity is central to the foundations of mathematics (B), including set theory, Cantor's work, and the crisis in foundations after 1900.
Section B.3 covers the crisis in foundations after 1900 including paradoxes and the metamathematical method that Godel's theorems epitomize.
Section B.3 discusses Logicism, Intuitionism, and Formalism as alternative positions on the foundations of mathematics.
Russell's Logicism and the crisis in foundations (B.3.c) are central to the foundations of mathematics.
Section A.2.c.iii covers the evolution of digital devices from the abacus to modern electronic digital computers.
Section A.2.c traces the evolution of computational devices from the abacus to modern computers, the history of computing.
Leibniz co-developed calculus (A.1.b.i) and contributed to the logical calculus, both central to this section.
Section A.1.b.i specifically names Newton as co-discoverer of calculus in the 17th century.
Number theory's historical development from Euclid through Gauss is part of the broader mathematical history in this section.
Section A.1.a.ii covers Greek and Hellenistic mathematics as part of the broader scientific achievements of the ancient world.
Combinatorics is part of the broader historical development of mathematical branches covered in section A.
Fractals represent a modern development in geometry and analysis relevant to 19th-20th century developments in A.1.b.iii.
Section A.2.d.vi lists topology as a modern idea in geometry, part of the historical development of mathematics.
The development of calculus and analysis is a major thread in the history of mathematics from Newton and Leibniz onward (A.1.b).
The relationship between pure and applied mathematics is part of the historical development, including the influence of physical science on analysis (A.1.b.iii).
Chaos theory is a 20th-century mathematical development relevant to the modern period developments in A.1.b.iii.
Section A.2.c.iii covers the evolution of digital devices from the abacus to modern computers, connecting to computer science foundations.
